title: Debris Hut (Cold Weather)
immediate_steps: Find a sturdy ridgepole (a long, strong branch) and rest one end on a stump or low tree fork
Lean thick branches against both sides of the ridgepole to create a rib-like frame
Pile at least 2-3 feet of dry leaves, pine needles, or grass over the frame for insulation
Stuff the inside of the hut with dry leaves to act as a mattress and blanket
Crawl inside and pull debris in to block the entrance
avoid: Do not build in a dry riverbed (flash flood risk)
Do not build directly under dead trees/branches ('widowmakers')
Do not make the inside too large (it won't retain body heat)

title: Lean-To Shelter (Reflector)
symptoms: Planning to build a fire for warmth
immediate_steps: Tie or wedge a strong horizontal pole between two trees
Lean branches against the pole on the windward side at a 45-degree angle
Weave smaller branches horizontally through the frame
Cover the roof with leaves, pine boughs, or a tarp to shed rain
Build a small fire on the open side so the lean-to reflects heat onto you
avoid: Do not build the fire too close to the shelter
Do not face the opening into the wind

title: Lost In Forest - Shelter Building
immediate_steps: Construct shelter before nightfall to protect against weather, wind and temperature changes
Select elevated ground that is dry and safe from falling branches or flooding
Use available materials such as leaves, branches, grass and cloth to create insulation
Keep the shelter compact to retain body heat
Place dry leaves or vegetation beneath the body to insulate from the cold ground
avoid: Avoid sleeping directly on cold ground

title: Shelter Building - Importance and Site Selection
immediate_steps: Select a safe location to protect the body from cold, rain, wind and extreme heat
Choose elevated ground that is dry and away from flood zones, falling branches and animal paths
avoid: Avoid valleys where cold air collects
Avoid areas near unstable trees or rocks

title: Shelter Building - Materials and Construction Basics
immediate_steps: Use natural materials such as branches, leaves, grass, mud, and bark
Use strong branches as the main frame structure
Use smaller sticks and leaves to provide insulation and cover
Keep the shelter compact to retain body heat
Build a simple lean-to or A-frame structure for quick protection
Always ensure the shelter is stable and does not collapse under wind or weight
avoid: Do not build a shelter that is too large, as it will fail to retain body heat

title: Shelter Building - Insulation and Weather Protection
immediate_steps: Use dry leaves, grass, and pine needles as insulation material between the body and the ground to reduce heat loss
Cover openings using leaves or branches to block wind and rain
Ensure there is enough ventilation to avoid suffocation
In cold environments, reduce air gaps to help retain warmth
In hot environments, increase ventilation to prevent overheating
avoid: Do not seal the shelter completely; suffocation is a severe risk if there is no ventilation

title: Shelter Building - Emergency Shelter Types
immediate_steps: Lean-to shelter: Built by leaning branches against a strong support like a tree or log
A-frame shelter: Made by forming a triangular structure using two main poles
Debris hut: Uses natural materials like leaves and branches to fully cover the body for insulation
Prioritize quick and simple structures over complex designs in emergencies
avoid: Do not waste time building complex structures if time and daylight are limited
